title: Headless podman login fails in CI with "error creating tmpdir: mkdir /run/user/1000: permission denied"
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

In my Jenkins CI we build container images and push them to our local container registry. This works most of the time. However, every now and then (a couple of times per day) the Jenkins build fails with the following error message just after executing `podman login`:
```
Error: error creating tmpdir: mkdir /run/user/1000: permission denied
script returned exit code 125
```

Why is that? I cannot see any pattern when and why this would happen. It just does on a (ir)regular basis.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Create Containerfile with content:
```docker
FROM busybox

ENTRYPOINT echo "Hello World"
```

2. Execute
```
cat /home/my_ci_user/harbor_robot_tokens/CI_Robot.txt | podman login --username robot\$CI_Robot --password-stdin harbor.my.local.domain.com
podman build -t my-awesome-image:latest
podman tag my-awesome-image:latest harbor.my.local.domain.com/my-project/my-awesome-image:latest
podman push harbor.my.local.domain.com/my-project/my-awesome-image:latest
```

**Describe the results you received:**

Build job fails just after the `podman login` command with an output:
```
Error: error creating tmpdir: mkdir /run/user/1000: permission denied
script returned exit code 125
```

**Describe the results you expected:**

Build job succeeds with output
```
Login Succeeded!
```
after the login command.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

Happens only a couple of times a day.

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:      2.2.1
API Version:  2.1.0
Go Version:   go1.15.2
Built:        Thu Jan  1 00:00:00 1970
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

```
apt list podman

podman/unknown 2.2.1~4 arm64
podman/unknown 2.2.1~4 armhf
```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide?**

Yes

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**
Jenkins Linux build slave on a virtual server on VMWare

comments.body: Did you enable to account to linger?
What exactly do you mean by that? Sorry, in case that is a stupid question. 🙈
As root: `loginctl enable-linger my_ci_user`. It's a systemd thing, and is pretty much necessary for running rootless podman.

Thank you for clarifying. The concept of `user lingering` is new to me. So far I have only written simple systemd service files.

I will try and see if enabling helps with my problem.
Just to make sure I understood the context correctly: The fact that my build only fails occasionally, has something to do with timing then? If the build takes too long, the user session is closed already? Or how would that lingering argument work?

I... don't really know. I think it might have to do with, if you're ssh'ed in as that account while the cron job is running, it'll work, but the cron job by itself, with no actual session login, makes everything fail. For me it's just superstition: any time I set up a new account anywhere having to do with systemd, I automatically run the magic `loginctl` command, and things work better.

Bottom line is, systemd kills processes that relate to a user account when the user logs out.  Enabling linger mode allows the processes to stay running.  Similarly if you want systemd to setup a user session at boot time, then  you need to enable it.  Podman running as a rootless user needs a user session.

Yeah, I understand that. However, in my case, while the build job is running and my build script is right in the middle of being executed, I can't see  how my Jenkins CI user would not be logged in. That just doesn't seem to make sense.

But I will give it a try over the next dew days. Let's see if that fixes my problems.

@svdHero my understanding is that systemd somehow distinguishes between "user processes that have come in via a console, or X11, or ssh" and "user processes run from cron or nohup'ed". For some reason, systemd thinks it is very important to kill user processes if there's no "session". For oldtime UNIX people this is incomprehensible and infuriating, but we have to live with it. The magic `loginctl` is the way to get a useful working system again. HTH.

Basically if the user session is not started using pam_systemd within the pam stack, then the session including /run/user/UID will not be setup properly.  Podman relies on this /run/user/UID directory existing to do it's thing.

I did the magical `loginctl enable-linger my_ci_user` and haven't encountered any permission problems anymore.
Thanks for helping, everyone. :+1:

I am running podman in WSL2 and I got this error after I've restarted the host. The solution for me was to run in powershell on the host the command: `podman machine start`

Maybe it will help someone that runs into this for the same reason as me.
